source.title: Network-level encoding of local neurotransmitters in cortical astrocytes

confidence_rationale: In vivo and ex vivo two-photon imaging in mice directly demonstrates that GABA and glutamate inputs to the same astrocyte network generate qualitatively different, distinguishable Ca responses -- specifically, propagative vs. non-propagative activity patterns -- over a minutes-long time course. This is direct experimental evidence that astrocyte Ca signaling is multi-dimensional, with at least two functionally distinct channels encoding different neurotransmitter identities.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Brief, subcellular GABA and glutamate inputs produce widespread, long-lasting Ca responses across the cortical astrocyte network. A key subset of Ca activity -- propagative activity -- differentiates astrocyte network responses to these two main neurotransmitters, and may influence responses to future inputs. Local, transient neurotransmitter inputs are encoded by broad cortical astrocyte networks over a minutes-long time course.
mapping.ree_translation: This paper provides direct experimental evidence for two-channel (at minimum) Ca multiplexing in the R-field: the same astrocyte network generates distinguishable Ca dynamics depending on whether it receives glutamate (excitatory, potentially harm-signaling) or GABA (inhibitory, potentially safety-signaling) input. For R(x,t) in REE, this means a scalar R conflates signals with opposite behavioral valence; a vector R with separate channels for excitatory vs. inhibitory input encoding would better represent the biological dimensionality of the signal.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The two-photon imaging measures population-level Ca dynamics averaged over many cells. The specific Ca subset (propagative activity) that differentiates GABA vs. glutamate responses is not yet fully characterized mechanistically -- it is defined operationally by its spatial spread pattern, not by a well-understood biophysical substrate. The mapping of GABA/glutamate distinction to harm/safety valence in REE is a theoretical extrapolation; astrocytes do not directly encode valence, they encode neurotransmitter identity.
mapping.source_context: Mouse cortex, ex vivo and in vivo two-photon astrocyte Ca imaging, photouncaging of GABA and glutamate at controlled spatiotemporal scales, Poskanzer lab (UCSF).

confidence_components.notes: Nature publication with rigorous experimental design (controlled photouncaging, both ex vivo and in vivo validation). Source quality is the highest of the Q-003 entries. Mapping fidelity is good but requires the additional conceptual step of linking neurotransmitter identity (GABA/glutamate) to REE valence channels. Transfer risk is relatively low because the in vivo cortical context is physiologically relevant.
